1:
"These things have I spoken unto you, that ye should not be offended." The witnesses read Christ's purpose in forewarning the disciples of coming persecution — to fortify them against stumbling when the hatred of the world breaks out.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the strengthening aim. Augustine: after the promise of the Spirit "to inspire them with strength to give witness," he adds this "that you should not be offended," for "when the love of God is shed" abroad the soul stands firm. Chrysostom recalls that the Jews had already agreed to expel any who confessed Christ (John 9:22). Augustine of Hippo notes he had "prepared them by his own example to become the more courageous."

The grammatical-historical readers fix the word. Barnes: "offend" here means "to stumble or fall — that is, to apostatize" (Matthew 5:29). Robertson: the purpose clause with "made to stumble" (skandalisthēte), "the stumblingblocks which trip up a disciple" (1 John 2:10). Ellicott refers it to "the things which he had just said" of the world's hatred and the Spirit's witness (John 15:18-27).

The Reformed and Puritan voices read the pastoral prudence. Calvin: "none of these warnings are superfluous; for, since wars and contests await them, it is necessary that they should be provided beforehand with the necessary arms" — and what he said to the disciples "is also spoken to us." Henry: "Christ dealt faithfully with his disciples... he told them the worst of it, that they might sit down and count the cost." (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown in the merged sources here is from another passage and does not bear on this verse.) So the witnesses agree: forewarning is itself a weapon — Christ arms his own against offence by telling them plainly what is to come, that the shock of persecution may not shake their faith (v.4).

The witnesses thus read the verse as the hinge of the discourse: having promised the Spirit and warned of the world's hatred, Christ gathers all into a single pastoral aim — that knowing beforehand, his own may stand and not stumble. Calvin's word holds: what was spoken to the eleven is spoken also to the church in every age that faces the same hatred (John 15:18).
2:
"They shall put you out of the synagogues: yea, the time cometh, that whosoever killeth you will think that he doeth God service." The witnesses read the double cost of discipleship — excommunication and martyrdom, the latter committed in the name of religion.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the terms. Robertson: "they will make you outcasts from the synagogues" (aposynagōgous, 9:22; 12:42). Ellicott: "will think that he offereth to God a sacrificial service" — the word "is the technical word for offering sacrifice," so murder is mistaken for worship. Barnes lists the persecutions to come (Acts 9:1-2; cf. Acts 26:9). Lightfoot notes the casting out was "from the whole congregation of Israel," as with the Samaritans.

The Reformed voice reads the trial to the conscience. Calvin: "it was no light offence... that they were to be banished like wicked men from the assembly of the godly," for believers face "not only persecutions, but ignominy and reproaches" (1 Corinthians 4:12-13). Gill recalls the law already made against confessors and its terror upon the people.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke notes that excommunication stripped them of substance and civil fellowship (Ezra 10:8; Hebrews 10:34). Wesley draws a grateful contrast: "blessed be God, the time is so far past, that those who bear the name of Christ do not now generally suppose they do him service by killing each other for a difference in opinion." So the witnesses agree: the deepest peril is not open hatred but persecution dressed as piety — men killing the saints and calling it sacrifice to God; the very horror Christ traces, in the next verse, to ignorance of the Father and the Son (v.3).

The witnesses thus expose the most dangerous form of persecution: not open enmity but zeal that mistakes murder for sacrifice, a peril Christ traces in the next verse to ignorance of God himself (v.3). Paul, who once thus 'verily thought' he ought to oppose the Name (Acts 26:9), became the standing proof that such sincerity is no innocence.
3:
"And these things will they do unto you, because they have not known the Father, nor me." The witnesses read the root of all religious persecution — ignorance of God as he is revealed in Christ.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the cause. Robertson: "definite reason for the religious hatred is ignorance of God and Christ" (John 15:21). Ellicott: "ignorance of God revealed in himself" — there is "a special force" in this, for "men think that in exclusion" of the saints they serve God, while in truth they know neither Father nor Son (1 Corinthians 2:8; 1 Timothy 1:13).

The Reformed voice draws the comfort and the warning. Calvin: "not without good reason does Christ frequently remind the apostles" that "there is only one reason why unbelievers are so greatly enraged against them — it is because they do not know God"; this is said "not to extenuate their guilt, but that the apostles may boldly despise their blind fury." Gill: it was "pure wilful ignorance of the Father and himself."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke states it as a maxim: "ignorance of the benevolence of God, and of the philanthropy of Christ, is the grand fountain whence all religious persecution and intolerance proceed." Wesley: "this is the true root of persecution in all its forms." So the witnesses agree: the persecutors' zeal, however sincere, springs from a fatal ignorance — they do not know the God they think they serve, for they have rejected the Son in whom alone the Father is known (v.2); and Christ tells his own this beforehand, the next verse adds, that the event may confirm rather than shake their faith (v.4).

The witnesses thus locate persecution's spring not in malice merely but in ignorance of the Father revealed in the Son — a verdict that both humbles the church (its enemies know not what they do) and steadies it (their fury, however sincere, is blind). To know God in Christ, the chapter implies, is the only cure for the spirit that persecutes (John 17:3).

Thus, across the schools, knowing God in Christ is named the only true cure for the spirit that persecutes (John 17:3).
4:
"But these things have I told you, that when the time shall come, ye may remember that I told you of them." The witnesses read Christ's reason for the forewarning — that the fulfilment, when it comes, will strengthen rather than shatter faith.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the aim. Barnes: "by calling to mind that he had foretold these things they would perceive that he was omniscient," and remember "the consolations" he had given. Robertson: the perfect "have I spoken" (a solemn repetition), with the indefinite "whenever their hour comes." Ellicott: "he strengthens them by forewarning" (John 13:19; John 14:29) — "when the persecution comes they will... find in it support for their faith and evidence of his presence."

The Eastern father reads the timing tenderly. Chrysostom: "great is the tyranny of despondency," and Christ gauges what they can bear; Clarke gives Christ's own reason — "I would not trouble you by speaking pointedly at first... lest you should be discouraged," but now declares it "because it is absolutely necessary that you should be put upon your guard." Wesley: "I did not tell you these things at the beginning, because I was with you — to bear the chief shock in my own person, and to screen you from it."

The Reformed voice reads the practical end. Calvin: "this is not a philosophy fitted only for a season of leisure, but adapted to practice and use"; he discourses on these matters "that they may actually demonstrate that they have not been taught in vain," laying up his words in their minds against the day of need. So the witnesses agree: Christ forewarns not to alarm but to fortify — withholding the full weight of it while he was present to shield them, and now arming them, so that the very arrival of persecution would become a proof of his foreknowledge and a comfort in their grief, which he turns to address next (v.5).

The witnesses thus read the forewarning as an act of love, not alarm: Christ tells the worst beforehand so that the fulfilment may confirm his foreknowledge and his presence rather than shake their faith. He had spared them the full weight while he was with them to bear the shock; now, as he goes, he arms them — and turns to address the grief his going has stirred (v.5).
5:
"But now I go my way to him that sent me; and none of you asketh me, Whither goest thou?" The witnesses read Christ's gentle reproof — the disciples, drowned in grief, fail to ask where he is going.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the correction. Chrysostom: "the disciples, not as yet perfected, being overcome by sorrow, our Lord blames and corrects them." Augustine notes the contrast that the Spirit, poured into the disciples "throughout the whole world," would reprove not one nation only.

The grammatical-historical readers reconcile the apparent puzzle. Clarke: Peter (John 13:36) and Thomas (John 14:5) had earlier asked "whither goest thou?" and been answered, "but now" none asks, "because their hearts were filled with sorrow" (v.6). Robertson: "now that they realize Jesus is going, the thoughts of the disciples turn on themselves and they cease asking." Barnes: "had they made the inquiry, he was ready to answer and to comfort them."

The Reformed voice reads the consolation behind the rebuke. Calvin: "by a very excellent consolation he assuages the grief which they might feel on account of his departure"; what would have become of them, called now "to severe and arduous battles," "if they had not known that Christ was in heaven, as the guardian" of their salvation. Wesley: none asks "now when it is most seasonable." So the witnesses agree: the reproof is tender — Christ would have them lift their eyes from their own loss to his going to the Father, which is the very ground of their comfort and the condition of the Spirit's coming (v.7).

The witnesses thus hear in the reproof a deeper consolation: Christ would lift the disciples' eyes from their own loss to his return to the Father, which is the very ground of their comfort and the condition of the Spirit's coming. Their failure to ask 'whither?' is the measure of a grief too self-absorbed to see the glory toward which he goes (v.6).

Their silence, the witnesses note, was not contentment but a grief that had eclipsed the very Lord who was its object (v.6).
6:
"But because I have said these things unto you, sorrow hath filled your heart." A brief verse, and the witnesses read the disciples' grief — so absorbing that it crowds out both inquiry and comfort.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the note. Robertson: "sorrow hath filled" (perfect active) — "they do not see their way to go on without Jesus"; the word for sorrow is used of them only in this chapter. Ellicott: "the thought of their own separation from him, and of the dark future... so filled their hearts that it left room for no thoughts of him, and the brightness of the glory to which he was returning."

Gill lists the causes of the sorrow — the hatred and persecution foretold, being put out of the synagogues, the loss of life, and "particularly" the loss of Christ's bodily presence. The grief is real and not rebuked as sin, but as a thing to be lifted above.

So the witnesses agree: the verse diagnoses the disciples' state — a sorrow so total that it had become self-absorbed, fixed on their own loss rather than on the Lord's gain in returning to the Father. It is the very condition Christ now begins to heal, turning their eyes from his departure to its benefit, for it is precisely his going that will bring the Comforter (v.7). The grief that filled their hearts (cf. v.22) will, he promises, be turned into a joy no man can take away.

The witnesses thus diagnose a sorrow so total that it crowded out both inquiry and comfort, fixed on their own loss rather than the Lord's gain. It is the very condition Christ now sets himself to heal, turning their eyes from his departure to its benefit — for it is precisely his going that brings the Comforter, and the grief that filled their hearts will be turned to a joy no man can take (v.22).

The grief is real and not rebuked as sin, but as a thing to be lifted above by the promise of the Comforter (v.7).
7:
"It is expedient for you that I go away: for if I go not away, the Comforter will not come unto you; but if I depart, I will send him unto you." The witnesses read the great paradox — Christ's departure is gain, for it brings the Spirit.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the claim. Robertson: sympherei hymin, "it is expedient for you" (the same phrase as Caiaphas's in John 11:50, but "for you"). Ellicott notes the emphatic "I, who know all," prefacing words "of strange sound for the ears of disciples." Barnes asks why the Spirit's presence is "more valuable than that of the Saviour himself," answering that by his departure Christ would make atonement, ascend, and send the Spirit to be everywhere present with all believers at once.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke reads the divine order: "by the order of God, the Holy Spirit cannot come... unless he first die" — the necessity of his death (Matthew 20:19) here implied. The Puritan Henry: as the prophets comforted the church with the promise of Messiah, "the Messiah being come," he comforts with the promise of the Spirit.

The Reformed voice reads the weaning. Calvin: Christ "testifies that his absence will be advantageous," making "a sort of oath," because "we are carnal, and nothing is more difficult than to tear from our minds this foolish inclination, by which we attempt to draw down Christ from heaven to us." Wesley sums the chapter's structure: it is expedient "in respect of the Comforter, and of me, and of the Father." So the witnesses agree: against every instinct, the bodily departure of Christ is the disciples' gain — for only so comes the Spirit, who will make Christ present to them and in them more intimately than his flesh ever could; and the Spirit's first work, the next verses unfold, will be to convict the world (v.8).

The witnesses thus press the great paradox against every carnal instinct: the bodily departure of Christ is the disciples' gain, for only so comes the Spirit, who makes Christ present in them more intimately than his flesh ever could. Calvin's image holds — we must be weaned from drawing Christ down to us, and learn to seek him where he is, that the Spirit's work may begin (v.8).
8:
"And when he is come, he will reprove the world of sin, and of righteousness, and of judgment." The witnesses read the Spirit's threefold work upon the world — and prefer "convince" or "convict" to "reprove."

The grammatical-historical readers fix the verb. Barnes: "reprove" means "to demonstrate by argument, to prove, to convince... and particularly to convince of crime" — "he will convince or convict the world of sin." Robertson: elegxei, "convicting by proof" (John 3:20; John 8:46); Jesus had begun this (John 7:7), but the Spirit will carry it on. Ellicott prefers "convince the world," the only Gospel parallels being Matthew 18:15 and Luke 3:19.

The Latin and Wesleyan-Arminian voices read the scope. Augustine of Hippo opens the question of what the threefold conviction means. Clarke: the Spirit "will demonstrate these matters so clearly as to leave no doubt," first to "the Jewish nation" (Acts 2), then "the Gentile world." Wesley marks the Spirit's "twofold office — toward the world" (convicting) and "toward believers" (guiding, v.12).

The Reformed voice reads the gift's excellence. Calvin: having promised the Spirit, Christ "praises the excellence of the gift from its effect," which will not only guide and protect the disciples but "subdue the world." Lightfoot adds the historical force — the Spirit, absent some four hundred years, poured out so wonderfully would "evince to the world that Jesus was the true Messiah." So the witnesses agree: the Spirit comes not only to comfort the church but to prosecute the world — to convict it, by irrefutable proof through the apostles' preaching, of three great matters which the next verses define (v.9).

The witnesses thus read the Spirit's coming as a prosecution as well as a comfort: he convicts the world by irrefutable proof through the apostles' preaching, beginning at Pentecost (Acts 2:37). The threefold conviction — of sin, righteousness, and judgment — which the next verses define, is the Spirit's answer to a world that condemned Christ, turning the verdict back upon his accusers.

The Spirit thus turns the world's own verdict against it, prosecuting where men had presumed to judge their Maker (John 7:7).
9:
"Of sin, because they believe not on me." The witnesses read the first conviction — and debate whether the sin is unbelief itself or sin in general, with unbelief as its root.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh it carefully. Ellicott: "this should not be interpreted... of the sin of unbelief, but of sin generally; unbelief in Christ is stated as the cause of sin" — sin being "the disordered action of powers that have lost their controlling principle," and Christ "the revelation of the Father." Barnes, by contrast, places the rejection of Christ first as "the sin of chief magnitude," the foremost of which men are guilty. Robertson: without this conviction men keep "a pride of intellectual superiority in refusing to believe."

The Reformed voice grounds conviction in this beginning. Calvin: "the conviction commences with the demonstration of sin; for the commencement of spiritual" life is to know our guilt. Gill applies it first to "the Jews, in disbelieving, rejecting, and crucifying Christ," the sin charged by Peter at Pentecost (Acts 2:36-37).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke ties it to prophecy and Pentecost: the sin of "not receiving me as the Messiah, though my mission was accredited by the very miracles the prophets foretold" (Isaiah 35:5-6). Wesley: "of sin — particularly of unbelief, which is the confluence of all sins." So the witnesses, whether stressing unbelief as the chief sin (Barnes, Wesley) or as the root and cause of all sin (Ellicott), agree that the Spirit's first work is to break the world's complacency by convicting it of its guilt in refusing Christ — the necessary beginning before righteousness can be sought (v.10).

The witnesses thus make the conviction of sin the necessary beginning: whether unbelief is named the chief sin or the root of all sin, the Spirit first breaks the world's complacency by showing its guilt in refusing Christ. Only the heart convicted of sin will hunger for the righteousness the next verse reveals (v.10); conviction is the doorway to grace.

Conviction of sin, the witnesses agree, is the doorway to grace, for none seeks the righteousness of Christ who is not first humbled (v.10).
10:
"Of righteousness, because I go to my Father, and ye see me no more." The witnesses read the second conviction — most taking "righteousness" as Christ's own vindicated innocence, proved by his going to the Father.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers fix the sense. Barnes: this "refers to the righteousness or innocence of Jesus himself" — condemned as guilty, yet the Spirit would convince the world "that he was innocent," his resurrection and ascension the proof. Clarke: "of my innocence and holiness, because I go to my Father; of which my resurrection and ascension shall be complete proofs," answering the charge that he was an impostor. Wesley: "of righteousness... but I could not go to him if I were not righteous."

Lightfoot raises the old question — "whether his personal and inherent, or his communicated and justifying righteousness" — and allows "both may be meant." Ellicott: the world is convinced of "the righteousness of Christ, not that of the world," the going to the Father being its demonstration.

The Reformed voice marks the sequence. Calvin: "we must attend to the succession of steps"; men "will never hunger and thirst for righteousness" until "moved by a conviction" of sin, after which the Spirit reveals the righteousness found in the ascended Christ. Robertson: "without Christ they lose the sense of righteousness," seen in "the new morals" of a Christless age. So the witnesses agree: the Spirit convicts the world that the Jesus it condemned as a sinner was in truth the Righteous One — vindicated by his return to the Father — so that true righteousness is found not in the world but in him; and the third conviction follows, of judgment (v.11).

The witnesses thus read the conviction of righteousness as the Spirit's vindication of the condemned Christ: the Jesus the world judged a sinner is proved the Righteous One by his return to the Father. True righteousness, the Spirit teaches, is found not in the world's self-justification but in the ascended Christ — a lesson sealed by his rising and pressed home at Pentecost (Acts 2:36).
11:
"Of judgment, because the prince of this world is judged." The witnesses read the third conviction — the Spirit demonstrating that Satan is condemned and his power broken by the cross.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the reference. Robertson: "the prince of this world hath been judged" (perfect passive, "he stands condemned"), as in John 12:31 and John 14:30 — "the sinful world is in his grip, but he will be cast out." Ellicott: the perfect marks "the completion of the condemnation"; "the prince of this world hath been and remaineth judged." Barnes: the death of Christ "was a judgment or a condemnation of Satan," proving that God "will execute justice on all his foes."

The Reformed voice and Lightfoot read the victory. Lightfoot: "the prince of this world was judged when our Saviour overcame him by the obedience of his death" (Hebrews 2:14), the first instance being the resurrection, the next the loosing of the Gentiles by the gospel. Calvin: the gospel's light "manifests that the world has been brought into a state of good order by the victory of Christ, by which he overturned the authority" of Satan.

The Wesleyan-Arminian voices read the dethroning and its limit. Clarke: Satan's "usurped power... now to be greatly restrained, and by and by totally destroyed" (Colossians 2:15; Revelation 12:10). Wesley: "the prince of this world is judged, and in consequence dethroned... yet those who reject the deliverance offered them will remain slaves of Satan still." So the witnesses agree: the Spirit convicts the world that the cross was the judgment of its prince — Satan condemned and his grip broken — so that men are without excuse if they remain under a defeated tyrant rather than coming to the victorious Christ (v.8).

The witnesses thus complete the threefold conviction: the cross was the judgment of the world's prince, Satan condemned and his grip broken (John 12:31). The Spirit demonstrates that men are without excuse who remain under a defeated tyrant rather than coming to the victorious Christ — yet, Wesley warns, those who reject the offered deliverance are his slaves still.
12:
"I have yet many things to say unto you, but ye cannot bear them now." The witnesses read the principle of progressive revelation — truth withheld until the disciples, taught by the Spirit, could receive it.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the meaning. Robertson: bastazein, "to bear" (Acts 15:10); "the untaught cannot get the full benefit of teaching" (1 Corinthians 3:1; Hebrews 5:11-14) — "the progressive nature of revelation is a necessity." Ellicott: the "many things" are those into which the Spirit will guide them (v.13), "parts of the revelation which the minds of the disciples are not yet fitted to receive." Lightfoot lists them — the Christian sabbath, the abolishing of the Jewish, the rejection of the nation, the calling of the Gentiles.

The Latin and Eastern fathers guard the verse from abuse. Theophylact: "all heretics, when their fables are" exposed, appeal to this — but Christ does not license new doctrines. Augustine of Hippo treats it as "a subject of needful inquiry." Hilary uses the context to fix the Spirit's procession — "sent by the Son, and proceeds from the Father."

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices read the weakness and the supply. Calvin: their "ignorance" still kept them "in perplexity," "the weakness of the flesh" obstructing them. Wesley: "concerning my passion, death, resurrection, and the consequences" — "these things we have, not in uncertain traditions, but in the Acts, the Epistles, and the Revelation." So the witnesses agree: revelation is progressive, fitted to the receiver's capacity; Christ withholds what they cannot yet bear, not to leave them in the dark but because the Spirit will soon lead them into the fulness of truth (v.13) — and this is no warrant for novel revelations beyond what the Spirit then gave the apostles.

The witnesses thus establish the principle of progressive revelation: truth is fitted to the receiver's capacity, withheld not to leave the disciples in the dark but until the Spirit can lead them into its fulness (v.13). The fathers rightly add that this is no licence for novel revelations beyond what the Spirit then gave the apostles — the 'many things' are the apostolic deposit, not later inventions.
13:
"Howbeit when he, the Spirit of truth, is come, he will guide you into all truth: for he shall not speak of himself." The witnesses read the great promise of the Spirit's guidance — and find in it the charter of apostolic inspiration.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scope. Barnes: "a full promise that they would be inspired and guided in founding the new church." Ellicott: "into all the truth" — "not that the Holy Spirit will fully guide them into truth, but that he will be their guide into the fulness of truth." Robertson notes the masculine demonstrative ekeinos for the Spirit, a mark of personality. Clarke: "he will consider your feeble infant state, and... lead and guide you" as a father a child.

The Latin father probes "shall not speak of himself." Augustine of Hippo treats it as "a subject of needful inquiry" — the Spirit speaks not independently but from the Father and the Son, the very harmony of the Triune working.

The Reformed and Eastern voices read the Spirit as the perfect Teacher. Calvin: the Spirit is "perfect Master," given "that they might deliver from hand to hand the wisdom which they had received from him." Cyril of Jerusalem: "the Father gives to the Son, and the Son shares with the Holy Ghost." (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown here is from another passage.) So the witnesses agree: the Spirit of truth will lead the apostles into the whole truth of the gospel, speaking not on his own authority but conveying what is the Father's and the Son's — the ground both of the New Testament's inspiration and of its perfect harmony with Christ, whom the Spirit comes to glorify (v.14).

The witnesses thus read the verse as the charter of apostolic inspiration: the Spirit of truth leads the apostles into the whole truth of the gospel, speaking not on his own authority but conveying what is the Father's and the Son's. It is at once the ground of the New Testament's inspiration and the safeguard of its perfect harmony with Christ, whom the Spirit comes to glorify (v.14).
14:
"He shall glorify me: for he shall receive of mine, and shall shew it unto you." The witnesses read the Spirit's central office — not to erect a new kingdom but to glorify Christ and unfold his riches.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the promise. Robertson: "this is the glory of the Holy Spirit, to glorify Jesus Christ"; "he shall take of mine" is "a definite promise of the Spirit's guidance in interpreting Christ," fulfilled in Peter's Pentecost sermon and in Paul. Ellicott: the future guidance of v.13 "will be the revelation of the many things of Christ himself which they cannot bear now." Barnes: the Spirit "shall receive his commission" from Christ to do his will.

The Reformed voice guards against a false notion of progress. Calvin: "the Spirit will not come to erect any new kingdom, but rather to confirm the glory given to him"; "many foolishly imagine that Christ taught only the first lessons, and then sent the disciples to a higher school" — making the gospel "of no greater value" — but the Spirit only confirms Christ.

The Eastern voices join the Triune harmony. Origen reflects on "the life which is Christ"; Cyril of Jerusalem: "the Father gives to the Son, and the Son shares with the Holy Ghost." Gill: the Spirit glorifies Christ "in the ministration of the Gospel," asserting his full deity and ascribing to him the works and worship of God. So the witnesses agree: the Spirit is no rival to Christ nor the herald of some teaching beyond him, but his interpreter and glorifier — taking what is Christ's and showing it to the church, which is why all true spiritual experience centres on Christ, not on novelties; and what is Christ's, the next verse explains, is the Father's own (v.15).

The witnesses thus fix the Spirit's central office: he is no rival to Christ nor herald of a teaching beyond him, but his interpreter and glorifier — taking what is Christ's and showing it to the church. Calvin's warning stands against every claim of a 'higher school' superseding the gospel: all true spiritual experience centres on Christ, and what is his, the next verse shows, is the Father's own (v.15).
15:
"All things that the Father hath are mine: therefore said I, that he shall take of mine, and shall shew it unto you." The witnesses read a sweeping claim of the Son's equality with the Father — and the ground of the Spirit's work.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers press the deity. Barnes: "no one could have said this who was not equal with the Father" (Matthew 28:18; Matthew 11:27); the union so intimate that "whatever was done in relation to the Son was also done in regard to the Father" (John 14:9). Clarke: "if Christ had not been equal to God, could he have said this without blasphemy?" — the Spirit being "the ambassador of the Son, as the Son is the ambassador of the Father." Wesley, tersely: "could any creature say this?"

The Reformed voice guards the order while affirming the equality. Calvin: lest it be thought Christ "took away from the Father what he claimed for himself, he acknowledges that he has received from the Father all that he communicates to us by the Spirit"; "he speaks in the person of the Mediator, for we must draw out of his fulness" (John 1:16). Robertson: this explains "how and why the Holy Spirit can reveal to the disciples" what they need.

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem joins it to the Triune giving. So the witnesses agree: the Spirit takes "of mine" precisely because all the Father's are the Son's — a claim no creature could make, asserting Christ's full deity, yet spoken "in the person of the Mediator" so that the Father remains the fountain; the whole securing that the Spirit's revelation is nothing less than the riches of God himself shown to the church. From this height Christ returns to the nearer matter of his departure and return (v.16).

The witnesses thus read the verse as a sweeping claim of the Son's equality with the Father, spoken yet 'in the person of the Mediator' so that the Father remains the fountain (John 1:16). The Spirit takes 'of mine' precisely because all the Father's are the Son's — a claim no creature could make, securing that the Spirit's revelation is nothing less than the riches of God himself shown to the church.
16:
"A little while, and ye shall not see me: and again, a little while, and ye shall see me, because I go to the Father." The witnesses read the riddling promise — the brief darkness of his death giving way to the joy of seeing him again.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the two intervals. Robertson: the first "little while" is "the brief period now till Christ's death" (John 7:33; John 13:33); the second, "the period between the death and the resurrection" (Friday to Sunday); "ye shall see me" of the spiritual and risen seeing (16:22). Barnes and Clarke: "hidden from view in the grave," then "I will rise and show myself to you."

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the purpose and the obscurity. Chrysostom: he "again depresses them" after comforting, "to accustom them" to bear his absence. Augustine of Hippo: the words "were so obscure to the disciples" that they puzzled over them (v.17). Lightfoot weighs whether the "seeing" is personal or virtual — "ye shall not see me personally, but virtually."

The Reformed voice reads the pastoral aim. Calvin: Christ "often forewarned the apostles of his departure, partly that they might bear it with greater courage, partly that they might desire more ardently the grace of the Spirit," of which they had little desire "so long as they had Christ present with them in body." Henry: he tells them "of the comfort he designed them." So the witnesses agree: the saying folds death and resurrection into two short "little whiles" — a brief sorrow swallowed by a returning joy — but its very obscurity, owing to the disciples' grief and dullness, sets them inquiring among themselves, which Christ will graciously answer (v.19).

The witnesses thus fold death and resurrection into two short 'little whiles' — a brief sorrow swallowed by a returning joy. Its very obscurity, owing to the disciples' grief and pre-Pentecost dullness, sets them inquiring among themselves, which Christ, reading their hearts, will graciously answer (v.19); the saying both tests and tenderly leads them.

The saying both tests and tenderly leads them, the obscurity itself driving them to the inquiry Christ longs to answer (v.19).
17:
"Then said some of his disciples among themselves, What is this that he saith unto us...?" The witnesses read the disciples' bewildered conferring over the riddle of the two "little whiles."

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scene. Robertson: "some of the disciples" (ellipsis of "some" before ek, as in John 7:40); "Jesus seemed to contradict himself, for the disciples took both verbs in the same sense and were still puzzled over the going to the Father" of John 14:3 — "but they talk to one another, not to Jesus."

Gill reads their state sympathetically: "before the effusion of the Spirit on them, they were attended with a great deal of ignorance," and "what through their being overwhelmed at this time with sorrow," they could not grasp it. The very confusion illustrates v.12 — there were yet things they could not bear.

So the witnesses agree: the disciples, dull with grief and lacking the Spirit not yet given, cannot penetrate the saying, and turn to one another rather than to the Lord. Their whispered perplexity is the honest measure of pre-Pentecost understanding — and it sets the stage for Christ, who reads their hearts (v.19), to answer the question they were afraid to ask. The scene quietly commends inquiry that is brought, at last, to Christ himself, and exposes the limits of a faith not yet illumined by the Spirit of truth (v.13).

The witnesses thus read the disciples' whispered perplexity as the honest measure of pre-Pentecost understanding: dull with grief and lacking the Spirit not yet given, they turn to one another rather than to the Lord. The scene illustrates v.12 — there were yet things they could not bear — and prepares for Christ to take up the question they were afraid to ask (v.19).

The scene quietly commends inquiry that is brought, at last, to Christ himself, the only resolver of his own dark sayings (v.25).

Their confusion, far from being concealed, is set down by the Evangelist as the honest record of disciples not yet illumined by the Spirit of truth (v.13).
18:
"They said therefore, What is this that he saith, A little while? we cannot tell what he saith." A brief verse, and the witnesses read the disciples' frank confession of bafflement.

The grammatical-historical reader fixes the moment. Robertson: "the questions to Jesus cease and the disciples frankly confess to each other their own ignorance" — "we know not what he saith." The repeated stumbling-point is the phrase "a little while" of v.16, which they cannot reconcile with his going to the Father.

Gill reads the universality of the perplexity: "one, and all of them; the inquiry became universal." None could penetrate the saying, for the Spirit who would guide them into all truth (v.13) was not yet given.

So the witnesses agree: the verse simply records the honest helplessness of the disciples before a saying too deep for their present state — a helplessness that, far from being concealed by the Evangelist, is set down plainly. It is the very condition Christ described in v.12, that there were things they could not yet bear; and it prepares the way for him, reading their hearts, to take up their unspoken question and answer it directly (v.19), turning their confusion toward the promise of a sorrow swallowed up in joy.

The witnesses thus record the disciples' frank, universal helplessness before a saying too deep for their present state — a helplessness the Evangelist sets down plainly rather than conceals. It is the very condition of v.12, and it prepares the way for Christ, reading their hearts, to turn their confusion toward the promise of sorrow swallowed up in joy (v.20).

It is the very condition Christ described in v.12, that there were things they could not yet bear, soon to be borne by the Spirit (v.13).

None could penetrate the saying, for the Spirit who would guide them into all truth was not yet given, and grief had dulled their hearts (v.6); the Evangelist sets down their helplessness without disguise, that we might see how far short understanding falls before the Spirit opens the word.
19:
"Now Jesus knew that they were desirous to ask him, and said unto them..." The witnesses read Christ's reading of their unspoken hearts — a quiet proof of his deity — and his gracious anticipation of their question.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the knowledge. Robertson: "Jesus perceived" (egnō) their desire to ask, the imperfect in indirect discourse "just like our English." Ellicott: "this spirit of inquiry which he reads in their hearts" (John 2:25; John 6:6) "he proceeds to answer" — "the purpose of his enigmatic saying has been accomplished": their attention is roused.

The Reformed voice draws the deity and the lesson. Gill: "this is a proof of Christ's deity, that he is the omniscient God who knows all things, what is in man, even the secrets of the heart" — not their whisperings only but "their secret desires to ask him." Calvin: "though sometimes the Lord appears to speak to the deaf, he at length cures the ignorance of his disciples"; our duty is that "our slowness of apprehension may not be accompanied by either pride or indolence, but... humble and desirous to learn."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: Jesus spoke "preventing their question." (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown here is from another passage.) So the witnesses agree: Christ, knowing the heart, takes up the very question the disciples were too perplexed or timid to voice — an act both of divine omniscience and of tender condescension, meeting their bewilderment (v.18) not with rebuke but with the promise that their coming sorrow would be turned to joy (v.20).

The witnesses thus read the verse as both a proof of deity and an act of grace: Christ, knowing the heart (John 2:25), takes up the very question the disciples were too perplexed or timid to voice, meeting their bewilderment not with rebuke but with the promise that their coming sorrow would be turned to joy (v.20).

His knowing the heart is, the Reformed voice insists, a clear proof that he is the omniscient God (John 2:25).
20:
"Verily, verily... ye shall weep and lament, but the world shall rejoice: and ye shall be sorrowful, but your sorrow shall be turned into joy." The witnesses read the great reversal — the disciples' grief at the cross transformed by the resurrection.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the contrast. Robertson: "ye shall weep and lament" (the loud lamentations of the east), "shall rejoice" (the world) in "violent contrast" — picture "the women on the way to the Cross" (Luke 23:27). Barnes: "at my crucifixion... the world" — "wicked men," the Jews who sought his death — "will rejoice that their object was obtained," but the disciples' sorrow "shall be turned into joy" at his rising (John 20:20).

The Reformed voice reads the purpose. Calvin: he shows why he foretold his departure and "added a promise about his speedy return" — that they might understand "the aid of the Spirit was highly necessary"; "a hard and severe temptation awaits you, for when I am removed by death, the world will proclaim its triumph."

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem joins the witness to eternal life. So the witnesses agree: the verse sets the brief, bitter sorrow of the cross — when the world gloats and the disciples weep — against the lasting joy of the resurrection that will swallow it up; the same reversal the next verse illustrates by the woman in travail, whose anguish is forgotten in the joy of birth (v.21). The grief is real but momentary; the joy, real and abiding.

The witnesses thus set the brief, bitter sorrow of the cross — when the world gloats and the disciples weep — against the lasting joy of the resurrection that swallows it up. The grief is real but momentary; the joy, real and abiding, as the next verse's image of the woman in travail will show, where the anguish itself brings forth the gladness (v.21).

The grief is real but momentary; the joy, real and abiding, born from the very anguish that precedes it (v.21).

The same reversal the next verse illustrates by the woman in travail, whose anguish is wholly forgotten in the joy of the child born to her (v.21).
21:
"A woman when she is in travail hath sorrow... but as soon as she is delivered... she remembereth no more the anguish, for joy that a man is born into the world." The witnesses read the homely illustration of sorrow swallowed in joy.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the figure. Robertson: "the woman," any woman; "whenever she is about to bear," a "common Old Testament image for pain." Ellicott multiplies the prophetic parallels (Isaiah 26:17-18; Isaiah 66:7-8; Jeremiah 4:31; Hosea 13:13). Clarke notes "a man is born" stands for "a human creature."

The Reformed voice draws the comparison out. Calvin: the figure confirms that not only will sorrow be followed by joy, "but also that it contains in itself the ground and occasion of" the joy — as a mother, the grief once past, gives herself wholly to gladness. Gill: "as it is with a woman in travail, so it was now with them."

So the witnesses agree: the travail-image teaches that the disciples' anguish at the cross is not merely followed by joy but is its very birth-pang — the suffering itself bringing forth the gladness of the resurrection. The pain is sharp but purposeful and brief; the joy, once the new life is born, so fills the heart that the anguish is "remembered no more." The next verse applies the figure directly: their present sorrow will give way to a joy no man can take from them (v.22).

The witnesses thus read the travail-image as teaching that the disciples' anguish at the cross is not merely followed by joy but is its very birth-pang — the suffering itself bringing forth the gladness of the resurrection. The pain is sharp but purposeful and brief; the joy, once the new life is born, so fills the heart that the anguish is remembered no more (v.22).

The pain is sharp but purposeful and brief, the joy so full that the anguish is remembered no more (v.22).

The next verse applies the figure directly: their present sorrow will give way to a joy no man can take from them (v.22).
22:
"And ye now therefore have sorrow: but I will see you again, and your heart shall rejoice, and your joy no man taketh from you." The witnesses read the application — a present grief, a promised reunion, and an unassailable joy.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the promise. Barnes: "after my resurrection"; the joy so grounded in the certainty of his rising that "neither threats nor persecutions... shall ever take it away" — "this prediction was remarkably fulfilled." Robertson: the metaphor "points to the resurrection of Jesus which did change the grief of the disciples to gladness." Clarke notes some read "shall take away" (future), the resurrection "so completely demonstrated... that their joy should be great and permanent."

The Reformed voice reads the perpetuity. Calvin: "the value of joy is greatly enhanced by its perpetuity"; the world's joy, sought "only in fading things," is soon lost, but this joy is secure, drawn from the risen Christ.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley guards against misuse: this "gives us no authority to assert all believers must come into a state of darkness; they never need lose their peace, or love, or the witness that they are children of God" — losing it only "through sin, or ignorance, or vehement temptation, or bodily disorder." So the witnesses agree: the disciples' present sorrow is real but temporary, to be turned at the resurrection into a joy so anchored in the living Christ that no enemy can wrest it away — the abiding gladness of those who have seen the Lord, and the firstfruits of the access in prayer the next verses unfold (v.23).

The witnesses thus read the disciples' present sorrow as real but temporary, to be turned at the resurrection into a joy so anchored in the living Christ that no enemy can wrest it away. It is the abiding gladness of those who have seen the Lord, and the firstfruits of the new access in prayer the next verses unfold (v.23).

It is the firstfruits of the new access in prayer that the following verses unfold, joy and asking bound together (v.24).
23:
"And in that day ye shall ask me nothing. Verily... Whatsoever ye shall ask the Father in my name, he will give it you." The witnesses read the new era of prayer — direct access to the Father in the name of the Son.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the double sense of "ask." Robertson: the first "ask" may mean "question" (no need to inquire, as in verses 19, 30, the Spirit now illumining them) or "request," the second clearly "request" in the Father's name. Ellicott takes "ask me nothing" of the Paraclete's full illumination, so that they "shall not need to ask the meaning" as before (Acts 1:6). Barnes sees both senses — they had depended on Christ present, now they will ask the Father.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read the day of the resurrection. Chrysostom: "in that day," when risen, "ye shall ask me nothing" — no longer "Show us the Father" (John 14:8). Augustine of Hippo treats the asking "in my name" as the great theme. The Puritan Henry distinguishes "asking by way of enquiry, the asking of the ignorant," from "asking by way of request."

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices read the new light and the open hand. Calvin: a "grace of the Spirit" by which they receive "so great light of understanding as would raise them to heavenly mysteries." Wesley: "ye shall not question me about anything... for you will know all things clearly," and "whatsoever ye shall ask, he will give it — our Lord here gives us a charte blanche." So the witnesses agree: in the age of the Spirit the disciples will both need fewer questions, being inwardly taught, and enjoy a new boldness — asking the Father directly in Christ's name, with the promise that he will give; a gift the next verse urges them to begin to use (v.24).

The witnesses thus read the age of the Spirit as one of both fewer questions, the disciples being inwardly taught, and a new boldness — asking the Father directly in Christ's name, with the promise that he will give. Wesley's 'charte blanche' is bounded only by the name in which they ask: whatsoever accords with Christ, the Father grants (v.24).
24:
"Hitherto have ye asked nothing in my name: ask, and ye shall receive, that your joy may be full." The witnesses read the invitation to a new prayer — in Christ's name — and its end in fulness of joy.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the "hitherto." Robertson: "up till now the disciples had not used Christ's name in prayer to the Father, but after the resurrection they are to do so" — "a distinct plea for parity with the Father and for worship like the Father." Barnes: "this great truth — that we must approach God" through Christ — had not yet been their practice. Ellicott ties the fulness of joy to v.22.

The Reformed voice and Lightfoot read the Mediator. Calvin: the fathers "were not accustomed to pray without a Mediator," trained by the high priest entering the holy place and the sacrifices; now that Mediator is openly Christ. Lightfoot refers the "nothing in my name" to "the extraordinary gifts of the Spirit" — they had asked nothing extraordinary in the name of Jesus the Messiah until now.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke: "ask in my name, and ye shall receive all the salvation ye thus request, the consequence of which shall be that your joy shall be full." So the witnesses agree: a new privilege of prayer opens — to ask the Father expressly in the name of the Son — and its purpose is not bare supply but the completion of the disciples' joy, the very joy promised at the resurrection (v.22). Prayer in Christ's name, the chapter teaches, is the appointed channel by which the believer's gladness is filled to the full.

The witnesses thus read the new privilege of prayer — to ask the Father expressly in the name of the Son — as ordered to fulness of joy. Prayer in Christ's name, the chapter teaches, is the appointed channel by which the believer's gladness is filled to the full, the very joy promised at the resurrection (v.22); the Mediator opens the way the fathers approached only in shadow.

The Mediator thus opens the way the fathers approached only in shadow, that the believer's joy may be full (v.23).
25:
"These things have I spoken unto you in proverbs: but the time cometh, when I shall no more speak unto you in proverbs, but I shall shew you plainly of the Father." The witnesses read the promise of fuller, clearer teaching after the resurrection and Pentecost.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the words. Robertson: "in proverbs" (paroimiais, John 10:6); "shall tell" (apangelō) "plainly" (parrēsia, John 7:13). Ellicott: "in parables," referring specially to what he said from v.16; "the hour would come when he would speak no more in parables, but tell them plainly of the Father." Clarke fixes "the time" as "the interval from his resurrection to his ascension," the forty days of instruction (Acts 1:3).

The Reformed voice reads the encouragement. Calvin: the intention is "to give courage to his disciples, that, entertaining good hopes of making better progress, they may not think the instruction useless," nor suppose "that Christ did not wish to be understood." Gill lists the things spoken obscurely — the Father, the mansions, the way, communion, the manifestation.

(A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown here is from another passage.) So the witnesses agree: Christ's present teaching, veiled in figure and met by the disciples' dullness, is not his last word; the day of the Spirit will bring a plain and open showing of the Father — fulfilled in the risen Lord's instruction and supremely in the Spirit's illumination (v.13). The obscurity is temporary; the promised clarity, the inheritance of the Spirit-taught church.

The witnesses thus read Christ's present teaching, veiled in figure and met by the disciples' dullness, as not his last word: the day of the Spirit will bring a plain and open showing of the Father, fulfilled in the risen Lord's instruction and supremely in the Spirit's illumination (v.13). The obscurity is temporary; the promised clarity, the inheritance of the Spirit-taught church.

The obscurity is temporary; the promised clarity, the inheritance of the Spirit-taught church (v.13).

Christ's intention, Calvin notes, is to give courage — that the disciples may not think the present teaching useless, nor suppose he did not wish to be understood (v.12).
26:
"At that day ye shall ask in my name: and I say not unto you, that I will pray the Father for you." The witnesses read a saying that, far from denying Christ's intercession, magnifies the Father's own love for believers.

The grammatical-historical readers guard against misreading. Robertson: "Christ did pray for the disciples" (John 14:16; 17:9) "and he prays also for sinners" (Luke 23:34; 1 John 2:1); here the point is "the special love of God for disciples of Jesus" (John 14:23). Barnes: elsewhere "Jesus ever liveth to make intercession" (Hebrews 7:25), so this means not that he will not pray, but that the Father needs no persuading.

The Reformed voice resolves the apparent contradiction. Calvin: there "appears to be a contradiction," but the meaning is that whatever they need, "God will refuse nothing that shall be asked of his Son" — the Father's readiness is such that Christ need not be represented as wringing favours from a reluctant God.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke and Wesley read the Father's free love. Clarke: "I need not tell you that I will continue your intercessor... besides, the Father himself needs no entreaty, for he loves you." Wesley: "this in nowise implies that he will not" pray; "the Father himself now loves you... because of the faith and love which he hath wrought in you." So the witnesses agree: the verse does not set aside Christ's priestly intercession but lifts the disciples' eyes to the Father's own delight in them — they come in the Son's name to a Father who already loves them, so that prayer is not the storming of a closed heart but the asking of an open one (v.27).

The witnesses thus guard the verse from misuse: it does not set aside Christ's priestly intercession (Heb 7:25) but lifts the disciples' eyes to the Father's own delight in them. They come in the Son's name to a Father who already loves them, so that prayer is not the storming of a closed heart but the asking of an open one (v.27).
27:
"For the Father himself loveth you, because ye have loved me, and have believed that I came out from God." The witnesses read the ground of the Father's love — the disciples' love for Christ and faith in his divine origin.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the words. Robertson: "loveth" (philei) "the word for warm and friendly love," used here of God's love for the disciples, where John 3:16 uses agapaō for his love for the world; "ye have loved me" (perfect, "loved and still love me warmly") "and have believed." Barnes and Ellicott refer it to John 14:21 and 14:23 — the communion between the Father and the heart that receives his love.

The Reformed voice reads the bond. Calvin: "the only bond of our union with God is to be united to Christ; and we are united to him by a faith which is not feigned, but which springs from sincere affection." Gill: the Father "loved them as well, and as much as the Son did, and of himself too," from everlasting, proved in giving his Son and in calling them by grace.

So the witnesses agree: the warrant for the believer's bold approach (v.26) is the Father's own warm affection — kindled toward those who love the Son and believe he came from God. The love is not earned but answered: God loves those who, by his grace, love and trust his Son. Faith in Christ's divine origin and love for his person are thus the marks of those whom the Father himself cherishes — the very ones Christ came forth from the Father to seek (v.28).

The witnesses thus read the warrant for bold approach as the Father's own warm affection, kindled toward those who love the Son and believe he came from God. The love is not earned but answered: God loves those who, by his grace, love and trust his Son — the very ones Christ came forth from the Father to seek (v.28).

The love is answered, not earned: God loves those who, by his grace, love and trust his Son (v.28).
28:
"I came forth from the Father, and am come into the world: again, I leave the world, and go to the Father." The witnesses read this as a fourfold summary of the whole gospel — and a fresh assertion of Christ's deity.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke gives the famous outline: "I came forth from the Father — with whom I existed from eternity in glory; am come into the world — by my incarnation; I leave the world — by my death; and go to the Father — by my ascension. These four words contain the whole economy of the gospel" and "a consummate abridgment of the Christian faith."

The grammatical-historical readers fix the terms. Robertson: "I came out from the Father" (aorist, "the Incarnation"), "am come" (perfect, "the Incarnation is now a permanent" fact). Barnes and Ellicott: he repeats with emphasis what the disciples now believe (v.27) — "heaven, and not earth, is my home," and he returns to the Father.

The Reformed voice reads the deity. Calvin: "this mode of expression draws our attention to the divine power which is in Christ"; faith "would not be steady, if it did not perceive his divine power; for his death and resurrection, the two pillars of faith, would be of little avail if heavenly power were not connected with them." Henry: he comforts them that "though he was leaving the world, he was returning to his Father." So the witnesses agree: in one sentence Christ traces his entire course — from the Father, into the world, out of the world, to the Father — a creed in miniature that grounds faith in his divine origin and destiny; the very summary that draws from the disciples their glad confession (v.29).

The witnesses thus read the verse as a creed in miniature — from the Father, into the world, out of the world, to the Father — grounding faith in Christ's divine origin and destiny. Calvin's note holds: faith rests on his divine power, without which his death and resurrection would avail nothing; and this summary is what draws from the disciples their glad confession (v.29).
29:
"His disciples said unto him, Lo, now speakest thou plainly, and speakest no proverb." The witnesses read the disciples' glad response — they feel the light breaking, and welcome the plain word.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the moment. Robertson: "no wayside saying, no dark saying" (John 10:6; v.25). Ellicott: "the emphasis is upon the word now" — Christ had said the hour would come when he would speak plainly (v.25), and "his last words have explained what they before could not" grasp. Barnes: their perplexity over v.16 is now resolved by v.28, "and they expressed themselves satisfied."

The Eastern and Latin fathers read their state. Chrysostom: "the disciples were so refreshed with the thought of being in favour with the Father, that they say they are sure he knows all things." Augustine of Hippo reflects on "the inward state of Christ's disciples" — children spoken to of great things in a way fitted to children.

The Reformed voice tempers their confidence. Calvin: "this shows how great was the efficacy of that consolation, for it suddenly brought into cheerfulness minds formerly broken"; yet "it is certain they did not yet understand fully the meaning of Christ's discourse" — "the mere odour of it refreshed them." So the witnesses agree: the disciples, cheered by the plain word about the Father, gladly declare their understanding — a real but immature faith, kindled more by relief than by full comprehension, which the next verse will voice as a confident creed (v.30), and which Christ will gently test (v.31).

The witnesses thus read the disciples' gladness as a real but immature faith, kindled more by relief at the plain word than by full comprehension. Calvin's caution holds — 'the mere odour' of the consolation refreshed them — and their confidence, voiced as a creed in the next verse (v.30), will be gently tested before the trial that scatters them.

Their confidence, voiced as a creed in the next verse, will be gently tested before the trial that scatters them (v.31).
30:
"Now are we sure that thou knowest all things... by this we believe that thou camest forth from God." The witnesses read the disciples' confession — drawn from Christ's reading of their unspoken hearts.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the ground of the confession. Robertson: they had failed to understand his plain words about going to the Father (v.5), "but Jesus read their very thoughts" (v.19), "and this fact seemed to open their minds." Barnes: their difficulty over v.16 they had discussed among themselves without telling him (John 16:17-19), so when he answered the unspoken question they were convinced of his omniscience. Ellicott: they see in his knowledge of their thoughts "the fulfilment of the promise" of v.23.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the echo: "thou knowest all things — even our hearts... by this we believe that thou camest forth from God," echoing back Christ's own words of v.27, "implying, We believe in God; we believe also in thee." Clarke: they own him "that God who searchest the heart."

So the witnesses agree: the disciples ground their faith in Christ's divine origin upon his omniscience — his knowing their hearts before they spoke. It is a true confession, rightly reasoned, that his searching of the heart proves him come from God; yet, as the next verse will show, it is a confidence riper in word than in the strength to stand, which Christ must lovingly probe before the trial that will scatter them (v.31).

The witnesses thus read the disciples' confession as rightly reasoned — Christ's searching of the heart proves him come from God — yet riper in word than in the strength to stand. It is a true faith resting on a real proof of his omniscience, which Christ must lovingly probe before the hour that will scatter them (v.31).

It is a true faith resting on a real proof of his omniscience, which Christ must yet lovingly probe (v.31).

It is a confidence riper in word than in the strength to stand, which the hour of scattering will soon expose (v.32).
31:
"Jesus answered them, Do ye now believe?" The witnesses read Christ's searching reply — neither denying their faith nor flattering it, but warning of the trial to come.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh whether it is question or statement. Ellicott: "the words do not necessarily ask a question," and a sense "more in harmony with the context" takes them as an assertion — "our Lord did not doubt their present faith" (John 17:8), "but he knew" what was coming. Robertson: "their belief in Christ was genuine, but perils await them of which they are ignorant. They are too self-confident as their despair at Christ's death shows." Barnes: the question was "to put them on a full examination of their hearts."

The Reformed voice reads the gentle correction. Calvin: "as the disciples were too highly pleased with themselves, Christ reminds them that, remembering their weakness, they ought rather to confine themselves within their own little capacity"; "we never are fully aware of... our great distance from the fulness of faith, till we come to some serious trial." Gill: not a denial of their faith but "a reproof... for their security, vain confidence and boasting."

(A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown here is from another passage.) So the witnesses agree: the disciples' faith is real but overconfident, untested by the cross; Christ neither crushes nor inflates it, but searches it — for genuine faith, on every reading, does not yet know its own weakness until trial reveals it. The warning prepares them for the scattering he immediately foretells (v.32).

The witnesses thus read Christ's reply as neither crushing nor inflating their faith but searching it: genuine faith does not yet know its own weakness until trial reveals it. The warning is an act of love, preparing them for the scattering he immediately foretells, that they may not despair when their confidence fails (v.32).

The warning is an act of love, that they may not despair when their confidence fails in the hour of trial (v.32).

Christ neither crushes nor inflates their faith but searches it, for genuine faith does not yet know its own weakness until trial reveals it (v.32).
32:
"Behold, the hour cometh, yea, is now come, that ye shall be scattered, every man to his own, and shall leave me alone: and yet I am not alone, because the Father is with me." The witnesses read the foretold desertion — and Christ's unbroken communion with the Father.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scene. Robertson: "yea, is come" (perfect), "the long-looked-for hour is so close that it has virtually begun" (John 17:1); the arrest is near. Barnes: "ye shall be scattered" (Matthew 26:31), "every man to his own home," supposing his work defeated. Clarke: "your faith will be then so shaken that ye shall run away, each striving to save himself."

The Reformed voice reads the undiminished dignity. Calvin: "this correction is added, to inform us that, when Christ is forsaken by men, he loses nothing of his dignity"; "his truth and glory are founded on himself," so that "if he is forsaken by the whole world, still he is in no degree impaired, because he is God." Gill notes the nearness — "within an hour."

So the witnesses agree: Christ foretells, with sorrowful precision, that the very disciples who just confessed him will scatter and abandon him in his hour — yet adds the great consolation that he is never truly alone, for the Father is with him. The desertion of men cannot break his communion with God; and in this he is both their warning, that self-confident faith fails under trial (v.31), and their model, that the soul kept in the Father is not alone though all forsake it. From this he turns to his closing word of peace (v.33).

The witnesses thus read the foretold desertion alongside its great consolation: the disciples who just confessed him will scatter, yet Christ is never truly alone, for the Father is with him. He is both their warning — that self-confident faith fails under trial — and their model, that the soul kept in the Father is not alone though all forsake it (v.33).

The soul kept in the Father, the witnesses agree, is not alone though all the world forsake it (v.33).
33:
"These things I have spoken unto you, that in me ye might have peace. In the world ye shall have tribulation: but be of good cheer; I have overcome the world." The witnesses read the grand conclusion of the upper-room discourse — peace in Christ amid tribulation, grounded in his victory.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the threefold close. Robertson: "that ye may keep on having peace in me" (John 14:27), "peace to be found nowhere save in me"; "be of good cheer" (tharseite), "courage in the face of danger"; "I have overcome the world" — the perfect of settled victory. Barnes: "in me — in my presence and the aid of the Holy Spirit"; "I have overcome the world" by vanquishing "the prince of this world" (John 12:31).

The Reformed voice reads the necessity and the comfort. Calvin: he proves how needful the consolations were, since "numerous distresses await them in the world"; "all believers ought to be convinced that their life is exposed to many afflictions, that they may be disposed to exercise patience." Gill takes "these things" of the whole discourse — his departure, the mansions, union with him, the persecutions.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke reads the gift: "the peace of God is ever to be understood as including all possible blessedness — light, strength, comfort, support, a sense of the divine favour." So the witnesses agree, and the discourse closes: Christ does not promise his own escape from tribulation but peace in him through it, and the courage that flows from his finished conquest of the world. The believer's calm rests not on calm circumstances but on a Saviour who has already overcome — the note of triumph on which Jesus turns from teaching to prayer (John 17:1).

The witnesses thus read the grand conclusion of the upper-room discourse: Christ promises not escape from tribulation but peace in him through it, and the courage that flows from his finished conquest of the world. The believer's calm rests not on calm circumstances but on a Saviour who has already overcome — the note of triumph on which Jesus turns from teaching to prayer (John 17:1).
